title: Yes, the Supreme Court can!
sub_topics: five legal requirements of due process
Special Sandiganbayan Division for Estrada plunder case
judicial reinterpretation in the digital and biotechnological age

stances.claim: Due process protects 'all persons' — citizen or alien, natural or juridical — through five legal requirements: impartial court, jurisdiction over subject matter and person, full opportunity to be heard, and judgment only after being heard.

Authoritarianism is not the solution to legal complexity — the Filipino people will not exchange hard-won freedoms for 'the harsh efficiencies of strongman rule.' Singapore and Malaysia are not models for the Philippines because martial law was tried here and failed.
stances.rhetorical_move: appeal to historical experience (martial law) + comparative rejection

stances.claim: An innovative and courageous judiciary, backed by a vigilant citizenry, can redefine due process and equal protection through judicially-invented classifications — jurisprudence 'must be stable but it cannot stand still.'
stances.rhetorical_move: judicial activism principle stated as aphorism

signature_phrases: hears before it condemns, proceeds upon inquiry and renders judgment only after trial
jurisprudence must be stable but it cannot stand still
the harsh efficiencies of strongman rule
decision_framework_signals: applies substantive/procedural due process framework as a teaching device
frames judicial activism as evolutive interpretation — the Court 'can' refresh ancient rights for new technological ages
rejects comparative authoritarianism by grounding in Filipino historical memory of martial law
notable_anecdotes: Senator Jinggoy Estrada and Ombudsman Morales resolving a due process complaint informally to avoid delay
